1: The seventh month (haššĕḇîʿî) — Tishri, September-October — was the most sacred month in the Israelite religious calendar: it contained the Feast of Trumpets (Numbers 29:1-6), the Day of Atonement (Lev 16; Numbers 29:7-11), and the Feast of Tabernacles (Leviticus 23:34-43; Numbers 29:12-38). The convergence of the community in Jerusalem in this month reflects deliberate religious calendaring: the returnees gather for the high holy days as soon as the community is settled. The phrase “as one man” (kĕʾîš ʾeḥāḏ) is used elsewhere for the unified assembly of Israel (Judges 20:1; 1 Samuel 11:7) — a phrase connoting both unity and totality.
2: “Then Jeshua the son of Jozadak, with his fellow priests, and Zerubbabel the son of Shealtiel with his kinsmen, arose and built the altar of the God of Israel.” The first act of the restored community is the rebuilding of the altar — before the city walls, before the temple building, before any other infrastructure. This priority follows a principle reflected in Ezekiel’s restoration vision (Ezek 40-48) and in the logic of the sacrificial system: the altar enables the offerings that establish the covenant relationship; the building that houses it is secondary. The Mosaic precedent: the altar at the base of Sinai preceded the tabernacle (Exodus 24:4; cf. Exod 40).
3: “They set the altar in its place... though fear was upon them because of the peoples of the lands.” The phrase “fear of the peoples of the lands” (bĕʾêmāh ʿālêhem mēʿammê hāʾărāṣôt) indicates the security threat from the surrounding non-Jewish population. The decision to establish the altar despite this fear follows the pattern of the wilderness generation: Israel continued its worship in the desert despite external threat. The altar’s divine protection was more reliable than any human fortification — the covenant relationship maintained through sacrifice was the community’s primary defense.
4: “They kept the Feast of Booths (sukkôt) as prescribed, and offered the daily burnt offerings by number according to the rule, as each day required.” The Feast of Tabernacles was the first festival celebrated in the restored community. Its choice is theologically apt: Tabernacles commemorated the wilderness wandering (Leviticus 23:42-43), and the returning exile community was again in a vulnerable, tent-dwelling situation awaiting the building of permanent structures. The prescription “as each day required” (kidbar yôm bĕyômô) emphasizes strict Torah compliance — the restored community does not innovate but adheres to Mosaic legislation.
5: “And after that, the continual burnt offerings (ʿōlat hattāmîḏ), the offerings at the new moon and at all the appointed feasts of YHWH.” The ʿōlat hattāmîd — the twice-daily burnt offering (morning and evening lamb, Exodus 29:38-42; Numbers 28:3-8) — was the heartbeat of the temple’s ritual life. The restoration of the tāmîḏ meant that YHWH’s worship was functioning again on a continuous, calendrical basis. The list (Tabernacles → new moons → appointed feasts → freewill offerings) progresses from the specific festival just celebrated to the full annual cycle, signaling the community’s intent to observe the entire liturgical calendar.
6: “From the first day of the seventh month they began to offer burnt offerings to YHWH, but the foundation of the temple of YHWH had not yet been laid.” The editorial note is significant: sacrifice precedes construction. The community does not wait for the temple building to be complete before restoring worship. This precedent — worship at an altar before any structure — appears earlier in Israel’s history (the patriarchal altars of Genesis 12:7-8; 13:18 etc.) and provides theological precedent for the NT principle that the community of worshipers is the temple rather than the building (1 Corinthians 3:16).
7: “They gave money to the masons and the carpenters, and food, drink, and oil to the Sidonians and Tyrians to bring cedar trees from Lebanon.” The Solomonic building pattern is deliberately replicated: Solomon also used Tyrian craftsmen and Lebanese cedar (1 Kings 5:1-12; 2 Chronicles 2:1-16). The imitation of Solomon’s temple construction method signals the second temple’s intention to recreate the glory of the first. The involvement of Sidonians and Tyrians also reflects the continued role of Phoenician craftsmen as the Mediterranean’s premier woodworkers and architects throughout the Iron Age and Persian period.
8: “In the second year of their coming to the house of God at Jerusalem, in the second month, Zerubbabel the son of Shealtiel and Jeshua the son of Jozadak made a beginning.” The second year, second month — the exact same timing as Solomon’s temple construction (1 Kings 6:1: “in the four hundred and eightieth year... in the second month”; 2 Chronicles 3:2). The Levites “from twenty years old and upward” supervise the work — a departure from the Mosaic age of 30 for Levitical service (Numbers 4:3), justified by the urgent need for temple workers and following the precedent of 1 Chronicles 23:24-27 where David lowered the age to 20.
9: “Jeshua with his sons and kinsmen, and Kadmiel and his sons, the sons of Judah, stood together to supervise the workmen.” The Levitical supervisors are named: two main families (Jeshua’s kin and Kadmiel’s kin) plus “the sons of Henadad, with their sons and kinsmen, the Levites” (Nehemiah 3:18, 24 confirm Henadad’s family as prominent Levitical builders). The Levitical oversight of construction follows the pattern established for the tabernacle (Numbers 4:5-33) and for temple construction (1 Chronicles 23:4).
10: “When the builders laid the foundation of the temple of YHWH, the priests in their vestments came forward with trumpets, and the Levites, the sons of Asaph, with cymbals, to praise YHWH.” The foundation-laying ceremony follows the Davidic music tradition: priests with silver trumpets (Numbers 10:8) and Asaphite Levites with cymbals — the same combination used when David brought the ark to Jerusalem (1 Chronicles 15:24, 28) and at Solomon’s temple dedication (2 Chronicles 5:12-13). Music marks the transition from profane space to sacred space; the ceremony transforms a building site into a holy precinct.
11: “They sang responsively, praising and giving thanks to YHWH, ‘For he is good, for his steadfast love (kî lĕʿōlām ḥasĕdô) endures forever toward Israel.’” The refrain is the liturgical formula of Ps 136 and the temple dedication (2 Chronicles 5:13; Ezra 7:3) — the same words that filled the first temple with the Shekinah cloud. The singing of this refrain at the second temple’s foundation is a deliberate act of covenant recollection: YHWH’s steadfast love that had sustained Israel through the Exodus now sustains them through the return from exile. The community hears its own story in the refrain’s eternal present tense (“endures forever”).
12: “But many of the priests and Levites and heads of fathers’ houses, old men who had seen the first house, wept with a loud voice.” The temple had been destroyed in 586 BCE; for those now approximately 586 + 538 = over 70 years of age (born before the destruction), or those who had witnessed it as children, the second temple’s modest foundation was a reminder of what had been lost. Haggai 2:3 directly addresses these weepers: “Who is left among you who saw this house in its former glory? How do you see it now? Is it not as nothing in your eyes?” The prophetic response will be that the second temple’s “latter glory shall be greater than the former” (Haggai 2:9).
13: “And the people could not distinguish the sound of the joyful shout from the sound of the people’s weeping, for the people shouted with a great shout, and the sound was heard far away.” The mixed response — joy from those celebrating restoration, grief from those remembering what was lost — defines the eschatological tension of the entire post-exilic period. The restoration is real but incomplete; the promise has been partially fulfilled. This ambiguity drives the prophetic ministry of Haggai and Zechariah, who acknowledge the grief while insisting on the coming greater glory. The mixed sound heard “far away” signals to the surrounding peoples that something significant has occurred in Jerusalem.
